Apart from above mentioned responsibilities each club is required to follow below mentioned 
tasks: 
• It is advisable to all clubs to show online presence on various social media handles. • All the clubs need to submit event information like Event name, summary, date, time, 
venue, list of attendees, winners, organizers, pictures etc. to Office for record within 7 
days of event date. • To recognize contribution by student members of each club, School of CS&AI will 
organize an event to distribute certificated of recognition to student member of 
executive committee of all clubs. • It is decided to have separate page for each club on school of CS&AI webpage at SR 
University web site. If already exists, then regularly update the page with recent 
information. • Suggesting the administration to improve the student amenities to improve their career 
and personality. • Helping the administration in smooth conduct of student activities on the campus. • Guiding the students to improve their technical, organizational, and managerial skills 
by organizing seminars / symposia / workshops etc. • Encouraging innovative and creative skills of the students. • Activities to improve the knowledge and skills of the students. • Arrange expert lectures from eminent persons in the society for career and personality 
development. • Club focusing on programming and coding will take one lab of competitive 
programming course.

How-to Presentations: Have an expert in a specific field demonstrate and teach. How-to’s can range 
from anything including “How to fix a resume” to “How to use Photoshop,” and more. The sky is the 
limit! Fundraising: Fundraising is a great way to earn money for your club to help with winner reward, 
promotional activities etc. Conferences/ Workshops: Sponsor or co-sponsor a conference/workshop with a local non-profit 
organization. A conference/workshop is an excellent platform for researchers in all current areas of 
information technology to present their latest research findings, as well as an open forum for networking 
and exchanging of ideas. Networking: Interact with other people to exchange information and develop contacts. Seminars: Seminars bring together small groups for recurring meetings, focusing each time on some 
particular subject, in which everyone present is requested to actively participate. This is often 
accomplished through an ongoing dialogue with a seminar leader or instructor, or through a more formal 
presentation of research. They are essentially a place where assigned readings are discussed, questions 
can be raised and debates can be conducted. They are relatively informal. High School Students Shadowing College Students: Pair high school students with college students. The undergraduate should invite the high school student to spend the day with him or her, attending 
classes and eating meals together. (High school vacation days work well.)  
College Students Shadowing Professionals: Pair college students with professionals in the students’ 
desired career field. A student can spend the day with the professional at his/her place of work. Club Posters: Gather a group of students to make posters advertising all of the schoolevents. Alternately, students may create posters advertising course offerings for the upcoming semester, 
focusing on the fact that just one computer science course can be beneficial regardless of the person’s 
major. Poster Session: Organize a small poster session for students who have conducted summer research and 
participated in internships. Banquet/Award Ceremonies: Reward academically outstanding students by recognizing them at an 
end-of-semester banquet. Rewards can include gift cards for local restaurants or stores, plaques, or 
certificates. Be sure to include at least one “most improved” award. Mentoring: Pair younger students with older students based on similar interests and hobbies. Make 
sure that someone in the group is made explicitly responsible for driving group activities, so momentum 
begins early and keeps going. Tutoring: If at all possible, find funding for departmental tutors. (In extreme cases, where no funding 
can be secured, ask student volunteers to tutor their peers in specific classes.) Tutors can sign up for 
time slots; the information can be emailed to majors; and students can meet the tutors in the computer 
labs or in special reserved rooms. Alternatively, your club may want to organize volunteers to tutor 
local high school students, using a location on the high school campus.

Make a Website: Make a website for your club. Include upcoming activities, pictures and descriptions 
of recent events, and biographies of successful people in a wide variety of interesting computing-related 
careers. Study Sessions: For classes, have students from the same classes can meet the night before a test to 
review the material and ask questions. For GRE or graduate school preparation, ask faculty members

Brain Games: Send out campus flyers from the club. The flyers should be filled with brain games and 
puzzles that exercise one’s problem solving skills. Be sure to include a line that says “If you like these 
brain games”  
Visit High Schools: Ask for student volunteers to visit local high schools to educate younger students 
about the field of computer science and its benefits. Call high schools to find out times and dates for 
college/career fairs or other counseling events. Offer to attend the event and represent computer science. Programming Contests: As a club event you may choose to hold a programming contest for 
introductory students only, with questions supplied by local professors. Invite advanced students to 
prepare for and to attend programming contests hosted by professional organizations. Showcase: Organize a short-series of workshops that happen every week or two, where faculty 
members (and perhaps PhD students) provide short, engaging presentations on their research, aimed at 
undeclared, exploring undergraduates. Open House: Hold an information session close to registration time for prospective students. Invite 
current computer science majors as well as professors in the department to mingle with possible future 
computer science majors. Offer the prospective students tours of the department and its spaces. Graduate School Applications: Faculty members, professionals, or graduate students give tips and 
advice to undergraduates who are filling out applications for graduate, professional school, 
assistantships, fellowships; writing personal statements and interviewing. Resume Building: This 
meeting is to help club members build a resume for potential internships. The workshop will organize 
group activities such as ice breakers and public speakings to prepare for interviews. Workshops: Hold periodic workshops for people in the community. Students can help citizens with 
any computer problems they may be having, or simply teach them to set up and use an email account. Additionally, students may help the unemployed or under-employed construct resumes and learn basic 
technology skills. Mock Interviews: Have professors or professionals from area businesses volunteer to participate in 
mock interviews for students contemplating graduate school or entering the work world. Panel Discussions and Roundtables: Invite three or four people from the technology workforce to 
speak with students about their careers and lives outside work. Database of Interview Questions: Construct a database of interview questions and add to the database 
as each year’s group of seniors interviews. The database may also include general tips and advice for 
interviews. Database of Research Opportunities: Compose a database which contains information about different 
research opportunities available to undergraduates. Database of Scholarships and Grants: Build a database of scholarship opportunities for people in 
computing. Encourage people to provide the names and contact information for scholarships they have 
applied for or received. Database of Internships: Have students submit reviews of their internships — location, specifics of 
the job, feedback, etc.

Achievement Parties: Celebrate when students or faculty members in the department gain special 
recognition. Examples include job offers, graduate school acceptance or receipt of an award of special 
significance. Club Activities Movie Night: People spend a simple, relaxing night with other people in the computer 
science department, watching movies and eating popcorn. Recognize scenes where errors demonstrate 
that the producer had no computing consultant present or ask students to silently record the errors and 
award a prize for the “best set of errors”. Movie night provides an opportunity for socializing and a 
break from studying. Meet the Faculty: Set up a luncheon date at the beginning of each school year and invite students and 
faculty to meet and socialize. Organize “how to succeed in computer science” discussions at these kinds 
of socials. 5. MILESTONE GUIDELINES

Who Can Participate? The SOCS&AI Student Clubs will consist of students in core committee. Members must 
complete an application and meet the eligibility requirements. We are looking for some 
dynamic, enthusiastic, and committed students to lead the Student Clubs for this year. Qualities 
necessary for any member on core committee are: 
Eligibility Criteria for Candidates  
 
• Candidate in no event shall have any academic backlogs. • The candidate shall not have been subject to any disciplinary action by the University 
authorities. Selection Criteria 
The ideal candidate should possess outstanding attributes in the following key areas:  
 Leadership Skills 
• Effectively lead and manage the club through a process of delegation, team-building, 
collaboration, and empowerment. • Foster the development of leadership skills in the club members. • Promote academic excellence through continued strengthening of the club related 
activities. • Select outstanding students for executive committee members. Executive Skills 
• Set appropriate priorities and goals for the club. • Coordinate closely with the administrative team to ensure unified and consistent 
messaging. • Develop a comprehensive strategy to facilitate coordination across the school system. • Engage in proactive forward planning to limit the need for reactive decision-making. • Position the school to take advantage of technological advances and opportunities in an 
ever-changing world. • Promote fiscal responsibility, effective resource allocation, and sound budget 
management. • Act decisively, ethically, and empathetically when there is a need to rapidly address 
difficult issues or crises; and accept full responsibility for those decisions. Advocacy Skills 
• Demonstrate commitment to the central importance of serving students at the school. • Project a positive image of the school and promote its brand to the community, state, 
nation, and world. • Successfully and creatively lead capital campaigns to attract the resources that are 
necessary for the club to continue to achieve excellence in its mission.

Introduction to the School
History
SREC was established in 2002 and is sponsored by the SR Educational 
Society. The institution has been ranked Number 91 in NIRF Engineering 
ranking category and 1st rank in self-financed private institutions in India by 
All India ranking for institutions innovation by MHRD. SR Engineering College 
has been given the status of private University by the State of Telangana and 
then named SR University. Campus
The SR University campus is located in Ananthasagar village of Hasanparthy 
Mandal near Hanamkonda, Telangana, India. It is in 150 acres, with both 
separate hostel facilities for boys and girls. There is a huge central library 
along with Indias largest Technology Business Incubator (TBI) in tier 2 cities. CS & AI

The Department of Computer Science and 
Artificial Intelligence at SR University inaugurated 
the GeeksforGeeks Community on 8th January 2025 at the

wider tech ecosystem.

Batch representatives serve as a crucial link 
between the student body and faculty, playing a 
vital role in maintaining smooth and effective 
communication. They act as the voice of their 
peers, 
ensuring 
that 
students’ 
opinions, 
concerns, and suggestions are communicated 
clearly to the faculty and administration. By 
addressing academic and administrative issues 
promptly, 
batch 
representatives 
contribute 
significantly 
to 
creating 
a 
supportive 
and 
inclusive learning environment. Beyond communication, they play a key part in 
fostering unity within the batch. They coordinate 
between different student groups, help in conflict 
resolution, and ensure that every student feels 
heard and valued. Their presence is essential in 
bridging gaps, whether it's clarifying doubts 
about 
coursework, 
representing 
collective 
feedback, 
or 
facilitating 
participation 
in 
institutional events and decision-making





. Improving and Demonstrating Leadership Skills

       Being a batch representative also provides a platform to strengthen one’s leadership abilities. One of the most important traits is active listening—truly understanding the needs and concerns of 
fellow students before acting on them. This builds trust and respect. Additionally, adaptability is 
critical in addressing the wide range of challenges that may arise, whether related to academic 
pressures, communication gaps, or interpersonal issues within the group. Furthermore, effective representatives foster a collaborative and motivating atmosphere. They lead 
by example, inspire teamwork, and encourage students to engage actively in academic and 
extracurricular activities. By promoting inclusiveness, empathy, and cooperation, they empower 
others to contribute meaningfully to the collective success of the batch. In essence, batch representatives not only help in enhancing the academic experience of their 
peers but also grow personally and professionally. The role nurtures critical leadership qualities 
such as responsibility, initiative, problem-solving, and emotional intelligence—traits that are 
invaluable both in academic life and beyond. BR Meetings

SR Engineering College has been honored with the Best Performing Academy award by 
the Cisco Networking Academy in collaboration with TASK. This prestigious recognition 
reflects the institution’s outstanding performance and unwavering commitment to 
excellence in the field of digital education. The award acknowledges the college’s 
consistent efforts in empowering students with cutting-edge networking skills and digital 
literacy. Through active participation in the Cisco Networking Academy programs, SR 
Engineering College has demonstrated a strong dedication to preparing students for the 
demands of the modern technological world. The college's curriculum integration, 
student involvement, and faculty support have played a crucial role in achieving this 
milestone. This achievement highlights the institution’s drive toward fostering industry-
ready graduates. Receiving this award is a testament to the impactful learning 
environment maintained by the college. The recognition was officially presented in the 
year 2025 by Shrikant Sinha, CEO of TASK. SR UNIVERSITY

GitHub Community 
Overview 
The GitHub Community at SR University is a student-led developer community focused on open-source contribution, 
collaborative coding, and professional development. The GitHub Community promotes the use of GitHub for version 
control, project management, and portfolio building. The GitHub Community works closely with the Coding Club and 
ACM-W Chapter to organize joint technical events. Vision and Mission 
Vision: To build a vibrant open-source culture at SR University where students actively contribute to global projects and 
build strong technical portfolios. Mission: 
• 
To introduce students to open-source development and Git workflows 
• 
To facilitate collaborative coding projects among SR University students 
• 
To prepare students for programs like Google Summer of Code and GitHub Externship 
• 
To bridge the gap between academic learning and industry practices through hands-on projects 
Objectives 
The GitHub Community pursues these objectives: 
• 
Educate students on Git version control and GitHub platform features 
• 
Organize workshops on open-source contribution workflows 
• 
Host collaborative coding sessions and pair programming events 
• 
Facilitate participation in global open-source programmes 
• 
Build a repository of student projects showcasing SR University talent 
Activities and Events 
The GitHub Community organizes: 
• 
Career Pathways and Resume Mastery Sessions: Collaborative workshops with ACM-W Chapter focusing on 
building technical resumes and GitHub portfolios 
• 
Open Source Contribution Drives: Guided sessions for first-time contributors to open-source projects 
• 
Git and GitHub Workshops: Training on version control, branching strategies, pull requests, and collaborative 
workflows 
• 
Portfolio Building Sessions: Guidance on creating impressive GitHub profiles for recruitment

• 
To build a community of cloud practitioners at SR University 
Objectives 
The Cloud Computing & DevOps Student Club aims to: 
• 
Provide hands-on training in AWS and other cloud platforms 
• 
Guide students toward cloud certification achievements 
• 
Organize hackathons and challenges focused on cloud-native applications 
• 
Connect students with cloud industry professionals 
• 
Enhance student visibility on professional networks through certified skills 
Activities and Events 
The Cloud Computing & DevOps Student Club conducts: 
• 
AWS GenAI Internal Challenge: Internal qualifying event for "Hack the Future with AWS" GenAI Hackathon, 
challenging students to build Generative AI applications using AWS PartyRock and Amazon Bedrock 
• 
Cloud Certification Guidance: Structured preparation for AWS Cloud Practitioner, Solutions Architect, and other 
certifications 
• 
Practical Lab Sessions: Hands-on training with AWS services, infrastructure as code, and deployment pipelines 
• 
DevOps Workshops: Training on CI/CD pipelines, containerization, Kubernetes, and automation tools 
• 
Cloud Architecture Sessions: Design and implementation of scalable cloud solutions 
• 
Industry Expert Talks: Sessions by cloud professionals from companies like Zynga 
Membership Details 
Membership is open to all SR University students interested in cloud technologies. Members receive guidance on 
certification paths, access to cloud labs, and participation in cloud-focused hackathons. Leadership and Faculty Coordinators

Executive Summary
SR University (SRU) is the first private university established in the state of Telangana, India. It was founded
under Sri Rajeshwara Educational Society, a 50-year-old educational conglomerate that governs 95
institutions across Telangana and Andhra Pradesh, catering to more than 90,000 students with a teaching
and non-teaching staff strength of over 17,000. SR University is located at Ananthasagar, Hasanparthy,
Warangal – 506371, Telangana, and is accessible at sru.edu.in. The University holds the 91st position in the National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF) Rankings and
is ranked 7th among private universities in India in the Times Higher Education (THE) World University
Rankings 2026, placed in the global band of 801 to 1000. It also holds the 23rd position in the UI GreenMetric
World University Rankings, 50th in THE Impact Rankings, and carries NBA Tier-1 accreditation for its
programs. The University has a student population of over 10,000 active learners from 16 countries, maintains over 80
global academic tie-ups, works with more than 150 recruiters, and reports a 97 percent employment rate
among its graduates. The 2024–25 placement batch recorded over 1,200 job offers with a highest package of
51 LPA offered by CISCO, an average package of 6.5 LPA, and a top 10-percentile average of 21 LPA. This handbook has been compiled from official sources published on the SR University website (sru.edu.in)
and the SR Innovation Exchange website (srix.in). It covers institutional background, academic schools,
programs, fee structures, admissions procedures, research activities, international collaborations, placement
outcomes, campus life, the Minors and Honours program, and the SRiX incubation centre. All information
reflects the content available as of February 2026.

in SRU curriculum enables students to opt for courses that spike their interest, and the university status
brought agility to initiate an array of new programs to make education more industry relevant. Second,
inter-departmental learning that breaks departmental silos, creating T-shaped professionals. Third, a vibrant
learning ecosystem where academic freedom enables faculty to try different teaching techniques and create
a productive learning environment. Fourth, diverse activities including international conferences, workshops
from global thought leaders, hackathons, and entrepreneurial initiatives that make the regional ecosystem
vibrant. Fifth, emphasis on entrepreneurship and research culture, with the university attracting significant
funding from sponsoring agencies and taking a strong stand on entrepreneurial and research culture. In terms of regional ecosystem engagement, the tri-city area of Warangal-Hanamkonda-Kazipet is a highly

innovative initiatives, quickly addressing regional needs in farming, textile, and incubation, and working to

SR University holds the 91st rank in the National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF) India Rankings. In
the Times Higher Education (THE) World University Rankings 2026, the University is placed in the global

Development Goals. SR University holds NBA Tier-1 accreditation for its engineering programs. The

2. Academic Schools and Centres
Source: https://sru.edu.in/schools/Academics
2.1 School of Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence
The School of Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence is one of the flagship schools at SR University. It
offers B.Tech programs in Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) in its core form and with specialisations
in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning, Cyber Security, Data Science, Gaming and ARVR, and
Robotics and Automation. The school also supports postgraduate and doctoral programs. Faculty profiles for
this school are available at https://sru.edu.in/faculty/deptFaculties/cse. 2.2 School of Engineering
The School of Engineering at SR University offers programs across four core engineering disciplines. In
Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE), programs are available in the core stream as well as
with specialisations in VLSI Design and Robotics and Automation. In Electrical and Electronics Engineering
(EEE), programs are available with specialisations in Renewable Energy, Electric Vehicles, and Robotics and
Automation, with the core EEE program offered in partnership with Siemens. In Mechanical Engineering,
programs are available with specialisations in Robotics and Automation and Smart Manufacturing, with the
core program offered in partnership with Cyient. In Civil Engineering, programs are available with a
specialisation in Robotics and Automation, with the core program offered in partnership with DivyaSree NSL
Infrastructure. Faculty profiles for ECE, EEE, Mechanical, and Civil are available on the respective faculty
pages of the university website. 2.3 Astra School of Business
The Astra School of Business at SR University offers undergraduate programs in Bachelor of Business
Administration (BBA) in the core form and with specialisations in Marketing, Finance, Operations
Management, International Business, and Business Analytics. At the postgraduate level, it offers the Master
of Business Administration (MBA). The school also participates in the International Business Accelerated
Program (IBAP) in collaboration with the University of Massachusetts Lowell, USA, which is a dual-degree
3+2 pathway combining BBA from SRU with MBA or MS in Finance or Business Analytics from UMass
Lowell. Faculty information is available at https://sru.edu.in/faculty/deptFaculties/Business. 2.4 School of Sciences and Humanities

3. Programs 2026

3.1 International Engineering Program (IEP)
The International Engineering Program (IEP) is offered in two disciplines: Computer Science and Engineering
(CSE) and Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE), both in collaboration with top United States
universities. The program partner universities are the University of Massachusetts Lowell, the University of
New Haven, the University of Missouri, and the University of Central Missouri. The IEP offers flexible study pathways. In the first pathway, a student studies at SR University for the first,
second, and third years and then transfers to a partner US university for the fourth year. In the second
pathway, the student studies at SR University for the first and second years and transfers to a partner US
university for the third and fourth years. An optional fifth year is available for students who wish to pursue a
Master of Science (MS) degree from a top US university. To be eligible for the IEP, a candidate must obtain
at least 70 percent marks in the subjects specified at the 10+2 level, must have studied Physics, Chemistry,
and Mathematics (PCM), and must qualify in the SRSAT, JEE-Main, a state-level engineering entrance exam
such as EAPCET or EAMCET, or demonstrate merit in Sports or Cultural Activities. 3.2 Bachelor of Science Advanced Honours — University of Melbourne
SR University has a formal collaborative program with the University of Melbourne, Australia, offering the
Bachelor of Science Advanced (Honours) degree with CRICOS code 112780B. This is a four-year program in
which students complete the first two years at the SR University campus in Warangal, India, and the final two
years at the University of Melbourne's Parkville campus in Melbourne, Australia. During the first two years at SR University, students complete compulsory foundation studies co-designed by
the University of Melbourne and SR University. This covers the four major disciplines of science:
mathematics, physics, chemistry, and biology. SR University provides all lectures, tutorials, practical classes,
and on-site support during these two years and conducts ongoing assessments such as practical reports and
essays. SR University awards a Diploma in B.Sc. after the second year. In the third and fourth years at the University of Melbourne, students select a major from those offered in the
Bachelor of Science Advanced (Honours) program and complete a minor research thesis in the final year. The University of Melbourne designs and monitors the curriculum, monitors learning outcomes and graduate
attributes, and awards the full degree upon completion. The fee structure for this program is as follows. For Years 1 and 2 at SR University, the tuition fee is Rs. 2,50,000 per year and the hostel fee is Rs.

1,00,000 per year. For Years 3 and 4 at the University of
Melbourne, fee details are available on the University of Melbourne website at https://study.unimelb.edu.au. To be eligible for entry into SR University for Years 1 and 2, a candidate must have completed 10+2 in any
science stream, preferably with mathematics but not mandatorily, with a minimum of 70 percent marks. To be
eligible for transfer to the University of Melbourne at the end of Year 2, the candidate must complete

majors are provided in Section 3.2 of this handbook. Career outcomes from this program include
opportunities across hospital settings, industrial research, scientific laboratories, coral reef ecology, executive

the top 10 universities worldwide for graduate employability according to QS Graduate Employability
Rankings 2022. 7.3 Features for International Students at SR University
SR University actively welcomes students from across the globe and is among the youngest private
universities in South India to achieve both national and global rankings. The University campus is culturally
diverse, with over 10,000 active students from 16 countries. Merit-based scholarships of up to 80 percent are
available for high-achieving international students. The curriculum is internationally friendly and aligned with
global education standards. Credit transfer options are available to support smooth mobility between SR
University and its international partner institutions. The campus offers over 100 events, technical activities,
and skill-building programs annually. The SRiX incubation centre within the campus provides international
students with opportunities for entrepreneurship, internships, and innovation.

10. Minors and Honours Programs
Source: https://sru.edu.in/minors_honours
10.1 Minor Degree Overview
Unlike conventional universities, SR University offers every undergraduate student the opportunity to earn a
Minor Degree by completing 16 additional credits from a discipline different from their primary major. The
Minor Degree allows students to pursue subjects that interest them on a personal level, even if those
subjects are unrelated to their primary program of study. Minors can be combined across completely different
fields — for example, a student majoring in Computer Science can minor in UI/UX Design, or a student
majoring in Civil Engineering can minor in Entrepreneurship. The structural flexibility of the Minor program at
SR University is designed to match student intellectual curiosity and to open opportunities for interdisciplinary
postgraduate programs and better placement outcomes. 10.2 Eligibility Rules and Process
A student is eligible for a UG degree with a Minor subject to the following conditions. For B.Tech students,
the candidate must have a CGPA of 7.0 or higher at the end of the fourth semester to register for the minor
program and must maintain a CGPA of 7.0 at the time of degree award. The student must successfully earn
a minimum of 16 additional credits by registering in courses offered by another department. Open electives
from the student's existing curriculum can be counted towards the minor as well. The additional credits must
be acquired by overloading during a regular semester or a summer semester. A minor course that is already
listed in the student's primary curriculum as a program core course cannot be opted for as a minor course,
since all minor courses must be earned as additional credits outside the primary curriculum. A minimum GPA
of 5.0 must be earned in all courses undertaken for the minor. The grades obtained in courses credited towards the minor award are not counted and shall have no
influence on the GPA or CGPA of the primary program. If a student fails to meet the CGPA requirement or
withdraws from the minor at any point after registration, the student will be dropped from the list of candidates
eligible for degree with minor and will receive only the primary degree. However, the additional courses
completed will be mentioned in the grade sheet. In addition to all eligibility requirements, a minor must be
selected outside the major department. Minors made up exclusively of courses from a single department
cannot be earned by students majoring in that department. For example, a student majoring in Business
Administration cannot minor in Marketing, Finance, or any other minor from the School of Business. A student must declare the option to pursue a minor in the SRAaP portal before the beginning of the third
year of enrolment in the case of B.Tech students, and before the beginning of the second year in the case of
BBA, IMBA, and B.Sc.

11. SRiX — SR Innovation Exchange
Source: https://www.srix.in
11.1 Overview and Mission
SRiX, or SR Innovation Exchange, is the Technology Business Incubator (TBI) of SR University, located
within the university campus at Warangal, Telangana. It is a Department of Science and Technology (DST)
TBI, meaning it operates under the recognition and support of the Government of India's DST. SRiX is an
open incubator that welcomes entrepreneurs from across the country and is not restricted to members of the
SR University community. SRiX nurtures startup ideas and technology innovations from the idea stage, provides the resources required
to build products and find customer acceptance, and supports startups to gear for growth through access to
global partners and investors. The incubator brings entrepreneurs, mentors, researchers, and academicians
together to create an inspiring ecosystem that transforms ideas into actions. The deep interaction between
the theory and practice of entrepreneurship at SRiX creates unique synergies and is intended to serve as an
active catalyst for the growth of Telangana's startup ecosystem. 11.2 Impact and Outcomes
As of the time of compiling this document, SRiX has supported over 171 startups, created over 741 jobs, and
funded 103 startups with approximately Rs. 13.57 Crore directed towards ideation, prototyping, and
seed-stage support. The startups funded and supported by SRiX have collectively raised over Rs. 76 Crore
from venture capital firms and angel investors. The international admissions page of SR University also
reports related figures of 170 plus startups, 430 plus jobs, over Rs. 36 Crore raised, over Rs. 7.2 Crore
disbursed, 25 plus incubated startups, 150 plus internships facilitated, and 800 plus innovators impacted. 11.3 Funding Programs at SRiX
SRiX administers several funding programs to support innovators and entrepreneurs at various stages. The
NIDHI PRAYAS program provides proof-of-concept funding for early-stage innovators who have a
technology idea they wish to validate. The NIDHI Seed Support System (NIDHI SSS) offers seed-stage
funding for startups that have validated their concept and are ready for product development and market
entry. The NIDHI EiR or Entrepreneur in Residence program supports experienced entrepreneurs who wish
to work within the incubator environment and contribute to its ecosystem. The NIDHI Accelerator program
offers structured accelerator support. The TIDE 2.0 program, funded by the Ministry of Electronics and
Information Technology (MeitY), provides support for technology-based entrepreneurship. The Startup India
Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) provides seed funding to early-stage startups. SRiX also facilitates connections
with Angel investors and Venture Capital (VC) firms for startups that are ready for equity-based investment. 11.4 Incubation Programs
SRiX operates four structured incubation and pre-incubation programs. The Pre-Incubation Program
provides structured support for individuals and teams at the ideation stage, helping them validate their ideas

Course Outcomes: The measurable statements that state what students are expected to 
learn in a course. Credit: Every course has a certain number of credits assigned to it depending upon 
the nature and importance of the course. The number of ‘Contact Hours’ in a week of a 
particular course determines its credit value. One credit is equivalent to one lecture/tutorial 
hour per week or two hours per week of self- learning/ practical/ project/ field work during 
a semester. Credit Transfer: The procedure of granting credit(s) to a student for course(s) undertaken 
at another institution/ University. Cumulative Grade Point Average (CGPA): It is a measure of the cumulative performance 
of a student over all the completed semesters. The CGPA is the ratio of total credit points 
secured by a student in various courses in all semesters and the sum of the total credits of 
all courses in all the semesters. It is expressed up to two decimal places. Curriculum: Curriculum is broadly defined as the totality of student experiences that 
occur in the educational process. It incorporates the planned sequence of instruction, 
instructional content, materials, resources, and processes for evaluating the attainment of 
Program Educational Objectives. Degree: A student who fulfills all the program requirements is eligible to receive a degree. Degree with Specialization: A student who fulfills the entire program requirements of 
the discipline and successfully completes a specified set of professional elective courses in 
a specialized area is eligible to receive a degree with specialization. Department: An academic entity that conducts relevant curricular and co-curricular 
activities, involving both teaching and non-teaching staff and other resources. Detention in a course: Student who does not obtain minimum prescribed attendance in 
a course shall be detained in that course. Dropping from the Semester: A student who does not want to register for the semester 
should do so in writing before commencement of the semester. Dual Degree: A dual degree program means that a student can pursue two different 
degrees from either the same university or two different universities or even from two 
different countries but at the same time. Elective Course: A course that can be chosen from a set of courses. An elective can be 
Program Elective, Open Elective and Specialization Elective. Engineering Sciences: The courses belonging to basic evolutionary aspects of engineering 
from Mechanical Sciences, Electrical Sciences, Civil, Computing etc. Evaluation: Evaluation is the process of judging the academic work done by the student 
in the courses. It is done through a combination of continuous in-semester assessment and 
end semester examinations. Grade: It is an index of the performance of the students in a said course. Grades are 
denoted by alphabets like A, B, C, D etc.

Identifying early on who will oversee archiving and destroying data and samples and 
establishing the methods for doing so is crucial in any research, researchers in SRU should 
focus on data processing with utmost care. Any research partnership agreement needs to 
have terms outlining the necessary arrangements for conducting the research. Researchers 
should keep detailed and precise records of all procedures taken, all permissions granted, 
all data collected, and all conclusions drawn. This is important for several reasons, including 
demonstrating a sound approach to research and preparing for questions about the study’s 
methods and results. Properly maintained notebooks can be used to prove who is entitled 
to what inventions. Data should be stored in a way that facilitates a comprehensive audit 
in retrospect. Backup plans and safe data storage are vital. It’s vital that you back up the 
original files and pictures. This is particularly important when modifying existing data or 
photos for better quality. When editing data or photographs, it is best to keep both the 
original and the modified version. Avoid simplifying or otherwise manipulating images. Secrecy is especially important when there is the potential for financial advantage. It is 
critical to have a reliable system for storing and retrieving research data. Primary research 
data must be protected in order to ensure its continued use. Before taking any data with 
them, researchers need to get permission from the Registrar via the department head. After publishing, the need to preserve data sources does not go away. All raw data should 
be recorded and stored in numbered, permanently bound laboratory notebooks, or in a 
specially designed electronic notebook. Cross-index the primary record with all ancillary 
materials (such as surveys, chart recordings, autoradiographs, and machine outputs) 
stored in a separate ring binder or folder. Data should be recorded into notebooks as 
soon as possible after being collected. The data should be uniquely identified by the date 
of record and/or the date of collection. Researchers should regularly have their notebooks 
checked and “signed-off” on by their supervisors to ensure that all relevant data is being 
documented. Regular backups of computer data should be made and saved in a secure 
yet easily accessible location, as on a disc. When it is practical to do so, a hard copy 
of important information should be made. Copies of the necessary software, notably the 
version used to process electronic data, must be kept with raw data. 1.3. Confidentiality in research and consultancy

According to SR University, plagiarism is “to offer work or ideas from another source as 
one’s own, with or without authorization of the source author(s), directly by verbatim 
copying or by usage of any AI software” (i.e., with or without permission from the original 
author). In certain cases, authorization might be provided for the usage of other sources 
through written permission which may not be considered as plagiarism. Self-plagiarism, a form of plagiarism, can also be committed by reusing one’s own work which 
is published in a source without proper citation to that source. It is considered plagiarism 
in educational content to use someone else’s idea or phrase without properly attributing 
the source of the work. It makes no difference if the source is an accomplished writer, 
another pupil, a web page without distinguished authorship, a website that sells research 
papers, or anyone else as well: Whether done knowingly or unintentionally, claiming credit 
for someone else’s work is considered as a form of academic theft and is despicable in all 
educational environments. 2.1 Forms of plagiarism
Verbatim: Quotations must be marked with quotation marks or indentation and fully cited. The reader must know which parts are your own and which are borrowed. 2.1.1	
Cut and paste: This type is defined when a person copy and paste the 
content from sources including internet, the list of sources must include references to 
Internet-derived information. Since Internet content is less likely to have been peer-
reviewed, it should be carefully assessed. 2.1.2	
Paraphrasing: Without proper citation, replicating someone else’s work by 
altering a few words and rearranging the sequence in which they appear or by closely 
following the framework of their argument to develop another related content. 2.1.3	
Collusion: This can include things like working together on a project without 
permission, failing to properly credit third-party assistance, or failing to adhere to 
guidelines for working in groups. You should know exactly how much teamwork is 
allowed and what responsibilities fall solely on one’s shoulders. 2.1.4	
Inaccurate citation: Always use the standard format for citations in the area 
one works in. The author should cite the sources not only at the end of the paper (in a 
bibliography), but also within the text itself (in a footnote). Also, one shouldn’t include 
books, articles, or websites in the bibliography or references that you didn’t consult.

Policy stated below in order to encourage the translation and commercialization of SRU’s 
research findings. This Policy establishes the rules for who owns, how that property is 
protected, and how it can be used for profit by SRU in connection with research projects. The report outlines SRU’s expectations for collaborating with industry and business 
organizations, as well as suggestions for dividing the financial rewards of commercializing 
intellectual property. Strategic Framework for Research and Intellectual Property Rights at SRU:

SRU Student Handbook 
221
Programs, Interview Preparation workshops and Alumni Support
•	 Students /are expected to adhere to a code of conduct during placement activities. This 
includes professional behavior, punctuality, and proper communication with both the CJAC 
and prospective employers. •	 Many a times it can happen that campus recruitment process can stretch till late night. All 
students have to inform this to their parents and have to arrange their own transportation. •	  Joining schedule completely rests with the company’s discretion and market scenario. All students need to join on time as per the communication received from the Company. •	 Sole discretion of CJAC to change the policies based on requirements. 3.2 Eligibility
•	
Students should register by submitting their information in the prescribed format 
provided by CJAC
•	
The entire requisite fee has been paid to the University. •	
Satisfactory conduct with no disciplinary action throughout the program. •	
Minimum 90% attendance in the proposed job ready trainings
•	
Successfully completed the semester examinations/course with a CGPA equivalent 
to 6.0 or higher, and with no backlogs
3.3 Placement Process
•	
Companies are invited through email, providing relevant information and job 
descriptions. •	
Companies fill out the annexure and submit it back to the CJAC via email. •	
Students will receive details of the job descriptions via email/ Portal/Whatsapp 
group/other platforms
•	
Data of interested candidates is shared with the company
•	
Pre-placement talk (PPT) & Placement drive dates are finalized through discussions 
between the company and the placement department. •	
Once confirmed by the company, students are notified of the PPT & Placement 
drive dates. •	
Selection process on placement drive date: Written Exam (Aptitude / Technical 
Test), Group Discussion, Technical Interview, HR Interview, Verification of Documents, 
Any other as per the company request. •	
Final result shared with CJAC and CJAC shares it with the students
3.4. Job Acceptance Policy:
•	
Institute follows ‘One Student–One Job’ policy. After being offered a job by any company, 
a student is not allowed to participate further in the placement process. However, students 
who have been selected by a company on a CTC (package) ‘X’ may be allowed to participate in 
selection process of one additional company offering a CTC package not less than ‘2 X’. •	
Students from Core branches from Non-CSE who are placed in software companies will 
be allowed to appear in Core companies of their branch. •	
Any student placed with non-technical profiles (Marketing, Content Development, Business 
Development etc.) will get opportunities in further campus drives with technical profiles. (Not

SRU Student Handbook 
222
applicable for management school students, placed with the Technical Sales profiles)
•	
Students may get multiple offers due to non-disclosure of the result in time by the 
company. •	
After having accepted a job offer, if a student does not wish to join a company due to 
genuine reasons such as pursuing        higher studies, then the student is bound to inform the 
CJAC and abide by the agreements/bonds they may have entered into with the company. 3.5. Early Joining:
•	
Companies may indicate early joining in their offer letters. If a student is allowed 
to join early, then student would have to give an undertaking whereby student would 
diligently undertake the remaining academic work and report to the concerned faculty 
member on the mutually agreed days. The student must manage the leave of absence from 
the company, to write their final examinations and complete other academic requirements 
in time. . •	
The University does not encourage early joining as it involves loss of academic 
credits which may potentially lead to incomplete course work. However, such early joining 
may be permitted depending on the merits of the case & with recommendation by the 
respective head. 3.6. DEBARMENT/BLACKLISTING GROUNDS FOR STUDENTS:
•	
If involved in any in-disciplinary activity or engaged in malpractices. •	
On account of Students giving wrong data/information in placement cell
•	
Students cannot drop out from selection process once shortlisted for further rounds 
after Aptitude Test. •	
Any kind of misbehavior/complaints reported by the company officials/volunteers
•	
Students are not allowed to contact Company / HR Delegates directly for any 
reason. •	
If a student is eligible as per the Job Description & chooses not to apply for 
three consecutive eligible job postings, then it may validly imply that the student is not 
interested in pursuing the placement assistance process. 3.7. Campus Recruitment Training:
Campus Recruitment Training is designed to help students improve the chances to get a 
right career opportunity and make industry ready. SR University shall assist participants with 
professional development and career advancement processes equipping students with the skills 
that are required for employment. Soft Skills Training, Resume and Cover Letter Development, 
Social Media Profile Generation, Art of Networking, Assessment, Mock Interviews and Group 
Discussions, Technical Trainings
